serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of cedar shoot seaweed fried rice is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at Flavours by Saute, Funan, Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice (mixed grain, vegetable) — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Cooked Brown Rice (NDB 20037), Cooked White Rice (NDB 20444), Quinoa cooked (NDB 20137), Seaweed dried (NDB 11667), Egg whole cooked (NDB 01129), Vegetable oil (NDB 04582); supplemented with HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice reference values for sodium and fat.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists for cedar shoot seaweed multi-grain fried rice in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition: approximately 55% mixed cooked rice (white, red, brown) and quinoa, 10% seaweed, 10% egg, 5% cedar shoot, 10% oil and seasoning, 10% moisture loss adjustment. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect typical restaurant stir-fry seasoning per HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice benchmarks (~450–600 mg/100g). Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — mixed grain fried rice analogous estimate; reference: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021). International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021: a systematic review. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cedar shoot seaweed multi-grain fried rice. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous mixed-grain fried rice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The inclusion of brown rice, red rice, and quinoa lowers the GI relative to plain white rice fried rice (GI ~72). Mixed grain rice dishes with high-fibre additions are consistently reported in the GI 50–58 range. GI 55 selected as a conservative upper-bound estimate for this multi-grain formulation. GL per serving = (55 × 24.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 47.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 480 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1680 mg sodium, representing 73% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce seasoning, as a full restaurant serving provides a substantial sodium load that may elevate blood pressure in sodium-sensitive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 55 (low-medium boundary) with a high glycemic load of approximately 47 per full serving due to large carbohydrate quantity; multi-grain composition (brown rice, red rice, quinoa) moderates the glycaemic response compared to plain white rice fried rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 150–200 g to reduce glycaemic load, pair with non-starchy vegetables or protein, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the multi-grain base provides slower glucose release than plain white rice but the total carbohydrate load remains significant at a full restaurant portion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.1 g/100g) and elevated sodium (480 mg/100g); seaweed and quinoa contribute beneficial micronutrients including potassium, magnesium, and plant-based omega-3 precursors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the sodium content in a full restaurant serving and consider a half-portion, while the multi-grain base and seaweed content provide dietary fibre and micronutrients that may support cardiovascular health when consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cedar Shoot (Xiang Chun) Seaweed Fried Rice is a contemporary Singaporean fusion fried rice dish served at Flavours by Saute, Funan, Singapore. The dish is built on a multi-grain base of white rice, red rice, brown rice, and quinoa, which collectively provide higher dietary fibre, protein, and micronutrient density compared to plain white rice. Cedar shoot (Toona sinensis, known in Chinese as 香椿 / xiāng chūn) is a fragrant spring vegetable with a distinctive savoury, slightly onion-like aroma used widely in Chinese cuisine. Seaweed adds umami depth, iodine, and additional dietary fibre. The rice is wok-stir-fried at high heat with egg, garlic, soy sauce, and vegetable oil, producing the characteristic wok hei (breath of the wok) flavour. The texture is fluffy with slightly chewy grains from the brown rice and quinoa, and the seaweed contributes a subtle oceanic note. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 168 kcal per 100 g with 5.2 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and 24.5 g carbohydrate. The multi-grain composition lowers the estimated glycaemic index to approximately 55 (low-medium boundary), making it a more glycaemically moderate option than plain white rice fried rice. Sodium is elevated at approximately 480 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is suitable for vegetarians and is halal-friendly. It is priced at SGD 13.80 per serving.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for cedar shoot seaweed multi-grain fried rice exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy component data supplemented by HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice sodium benchmarks. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mixed-grain fried rice references. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked brown rice (NDB 20037), cooked white rice (NDB 20444), cooked quinoa (NDB 20137), dried seaweed (NDB 11667), whole cooked egg (NDB 01129), and vegetable oil (NDB 04582). No exact match exists in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium is adjusted to approximately 480 mg/100g to reflect typical restaurant stir-fry soy sauce seasoning, consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice benchmarks. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on mixed-grain fried rice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; gi_category is 'low' per the Veritas threshold (GI ≤ 55 = low). 4. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g × serving_g) / 100 = (55 × 24.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 47.1. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in vegetable oil stir-fry preparations per USDA data. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 7. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on typical restaurant fried rice portion sizes in Singapore; no official declared weight is available from the restaurant. 8. The dish is classified as vegetarian (contains egg) and halal-friendly; kosher status is unverified. 9. Cuisine tags in the source record (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) appear to reflect the restaurant's broader menu rather than this specific dish and have not been carried forward. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002694 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific dish.
